title: 🎙️ Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: wildfire detection tech and BC Wildfire monitoring systems, satellite/drone surveillance for Chilcotin wilderness, wildlife tracking and conservation technology, watershed sensors and salmon habitat monitoring, weather stations and climate tracking in backcountry, avalanche forecasting tech, trail mapping and GPS systems, outdoor recreation apps and connectivity, backcountry communication devices (satellite messengers, Meshtastic), flood/drought prediction systems, remote cameras for wildlife research, and how technology helps manage and protect Cariboo wild spaces. Prefer articles that bridge wilderness/conservation AND technology. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): prehistoric and ancient biology (fossils, palaeontology, deep-time creatures) with no contemporary conservation technology angle — these belong to Sunday's Science theme; AI and data centre infrastructure stories about business strategy, investment, or corporate politics rather than direct wilderness, ecosystem, or land-use impact in BC; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime news with no outdoor or conservation relevance; consumer tech reviews and financial market stories; general political and judicial news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] NASA’s Webb telescope discovers giant planet hiding in one of astronomy’s most famous systems
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

A giant planet had been hiding inside one of astronomy’s most closely studied planetary systems, concealed by a bright disk of cosmic dust. Webb revealed Beta Pictoris d through atmospheric traces of carbon monoxide, water vapor, and methane, showcasing a promising new method for finding elusive exoplanets.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The Mary Pit Head in Fife, Scotland
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. The concrete frame of the No 2 Pit at Mary Colliery, informally known as "Big Mary" (to differentiate from "Wee Mary", a nearby competitor), remains one of only two reminders of the coal mines in this area, standing 80 foot high.

Adjacent to the structure is a small outdoor exhibit which showcases equipment from the time, such as coal-cutters, hydraulic roof supports, and carts (both for coal and for miners) that were used in the pit. The 'cage' that lowered miners into the pit is also displayed.

Additionally, there is a preserved locomotive steam engine, Andrew Barclay Works No 2259 No 30 0-4-0ST, present which can be explorer. It is typical of the National Coal Board engines that were used in collieries like this.

A noticeboard nearby also details the known deaths and injuries that occurred during the operation of the pit, sourced from historic newspapers, of which there were sadly many.
items.summary: The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. The concrete frame of the No 2 Pit at Mary Colliery, informally known as "Big Mary" (to differentiate from "Wee Mary", a nearby competitor), remains one of only two reminders of the coal mines in this area, standing 80
items._excerpt: The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. items.title: [Kagi Small Web] Instagram without* scrolling
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

I used to take my online privacy really seriously, having no social media, not using google or its services, using fake email addresses and fake names, always erasing cookies and site data, all the protections one can take to be tracked as little as possible.

But sharing my life is something that I love, and I don't want it to be private. I find it so awesome that thanks to internet, random people can know what you are doing and who you are. What I don't like about Instagram and other social medias is that I'm giving my data away for free to big corporations and they can do whatever they want with it. To put it another way, I'm not scared about someone random knowing where I live and what I like. Rather I'm upset that people are making money over where I live and what I like.

So lately, I caved in. I stopped making all these efforts to protect my online life because they were tiring, isolating and stripping me of something I liked. And for a few weeks now, I've been using Instagram again and it has been wonderful!

This has made me question myself about why my life with social medias is significantly better when most people seem to say that social media are making their life miserable. And I've come to the conclusion that this is just a question of how you interact with it. Since I haven't had Instagram for years, I have lost the habit of doomscrolling, and I never got into short form content cause it scares me too much. (It's way too addictive and I know I would be stuck like a slug to a window in a matter of minutes). This means that the way I use Instagram now is pretty positive and healthy. I watch stories, catch up on what the few people I follow have posted (Cause there thankfully still is a "following" tab) and post my own pictures or stories. Then I'm done, I close the app and go on with my day, I'm never trapped in the cycle of eternal scrolling since I don't even start scrolling at all.

This has meant that discovery has been happening through people's stories. I see they repost something and get intrigued, so I go on the account that posted the thing initially and sometimes, I follow them. This is in my opinion a great way to organically discover relevant content without letting the algorithms decide for you. Another side benefit from this way of using Instagram is that I feel way less lonely and isolated than I've felt for years. That's because before, the only way I had to find out what events were happening around me was through family and friends. That meant I was missing out on all the events they weren't a part of, and that's quite a lot of events! Since getting back on Instagram, I've had several moments where I simply look at stories and they share some gathering that is happening that week and it gives me opportunities to go and engage with other like minded humans and meet lovely people.

Overall, this has made me reconsider my position on social media a bit. I used to think they were like a bomb, nothing good comes out of them and it only profits rich assholes. But now I see them more like a knife. Very dangerous if you aren't careful but also extremely useful when used for the right reasons. Of course it still profits rich assholes (and that sucks), but it also profits me! And that's a small victory I'm more than happy to take :))

What's your opinion on social medias? Send me a mail so we can talk about it :))
items.summary: I used to take my online privacy really seriously, having no social media, not using google or its services, using fake email addresses and fake names, always erasing cookies and site data, all the protections one can take to be tracked as little as possible. But sharing my life is something that I
items._excerpt: I used to take my online privacy really seriously, having no social media, not using google or its services, using fake email addresses and fake names, always erasing cookies and site data, all the protections one can take to be tracked as little as possible. items.title: [ScienceDaily] Common antidepressant may ease long COVID’s crushing fatigue
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

A low-cost antidepressant may offer new hope for people struggling with long COVID fatigue. In a randomized clinical trial involving 399 adults, fluvoxamine significantly reduced fatigue and improved quality of life compared with a placebo, making it one of the first medications to show meaningful benefits for this disabling condition.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Enter Rachel Lichtman's groovy, shag-carpeted world of Golden Sands
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

I just discovered, through Frank Santopadre's podcast Fun for All Ages, the radio and television universe of artist Rachel Lichtman's imagined 1960s/1970s town of Golden Sands.

It's a surreal town stuck in a mid-century far out brown-and-orange aesthetic. Lichtman creates parody television commercials, and print and radio ads from the town of Golden Sands, as well as opening credit theme song videos from the town's television station, Programme 4. — Read the rest

The post Enter Rachel Lichtman's groovy, shag-carpeted world of Golden Sands appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] The Payphone at the Edge of the Forest in Altadena, California
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

At the edge of the forest in Altadena, a payphone.

Not just any payphone, a working payphone.

 A relic like this would be easy to miss anywhere in Los Angeles. But here, at the edge of the Angeles National Forest, she stands out, a metal box of human nostalgia set against brush and trees.

Emergency-minded—intended for people coming on and off the rugged trail—it’s become a beloved part of the local landscape over time; the kind of object people notice, photograph, and review online (yes, it has Google reviews).

But look closer and there’s something else: a sticker inviting visitors to say goodbye.

This is The Goodbye Line, a guerrilla, participatory art project and human archive created by Adam Trunell and Alexis Wood. The project uses working payphones as physical portals where strangers can leave the goodbyes they never got to say.

Some calls are for loved ones. Some are for former selves. Some are for pets, dreams, places, or strangers who left before anything could be said. 

The line does not answer back. It just listens.

 The project has since overtaken payphones across the world, but the Altadena phone was its first. A place where a throwaway hunk of human interaction became something softer and stranger: a portal to our deepest humanity. 

If you find yourself at the edge of the forest with a lingering goodbye, pick up the phone and leave it there.
items.summary: At the edge of the forest in Altadena, a payphone. Not just any payphone, a working payphone. A relic like this would be easy to miss anywhere in Los Angeles. But here, at the edge of the Angeles National Forest, she stands out, a metal box of human nostalgia set against brush and trees.

items.title: [Animikii News River] "AI moves fast. Human hearts move slow."
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

News River Express

News River Express is back with three articles worth your time, plus a bit of our own news.

We built this summer format so we can spend more time outside with our plants and our people, and still get the best links to your inbox.

This week is the topic everyone is talking about: AI, and where it meets Indigenous Data Sovereignty.

How Indigenous technologists are building data sovereignty into AI

BetaKit

Our CEO and Co-Founder Jeff Ward spoke at Mila's Indigenous AI Gathering in Montréal. The line that stayed with him came from Canada's Minister of AI and Digital Innovation, Evan Solomon:

"AI moves fast. Human hearts move slow."

The Minister came to an Indigenous-led space to present and to listen, weeks after Canada's national AI strategy launched. That's where trust starts, and it's how we build at Animikii: technology made with love starts with human hearts and lets love set the pace.

The BetaKit piece covers some of the conversations. Participants in the Indigenous Pathfinders in AI program pitched tools that keep data inside the community network. Diné geneticist Krystal Tsosie put the stakes plainly:

"[Data] determines what the system sees, what it optimizes for, who benefits, and who is harmed. Our community's data is already on this pathway, and the question is whether or not our community governs what it becomes."

Data centers are booming. Indigenous leaders want help protecting their lands.

Grist

AI infrastructure is hungry for land, energy, and water, and Indigenous delegates took that to the UN's Expert Mechanism on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples. A single hyperscale data centre can draw as much electricity as 100,000 households. Delegates called for free, prior, and informed consent, and some US tribes have passed outright moratoriums. As Sámi Parliament member Maren Storslett said:

"We must not only ask what AI can do, but what it should do."

Respecting First Nations knowledge on Wikimedia platforms

Wikimedia Australia

Wikimedia Australia and the Indigenous-owned firm Terri Janke and Company have released a guide for handling Indigenous knowledge across Wikipedia, Commons, and Wikidata. It matters for AI because Wikimedia trains many large language models, so errors and colonial framing scale with them. The guide uses a risk-assessment approach built on one question: who has the cultural authority to tell this story? As Terri Janke and Company's Shevaun Wright puts it:

"It's not about content moderation, but about content integrity."

And in Animikii news: we are testing ethical AI with an Indigenous community

We've partnered with CEIMIA on a first practical use case for ethical AI and data governance with an Indigenous community in Canada. It pairs two tools: our Niiwin platform, and the Data Roadmap CEIMIA built for the Global Partnership on AI.

Most talk about responsible AI stays at the level of principles. This pilot asks what those principles look like when a community runs them on its own data, with tools it controls. Niiwin carries OCAP®, CARE, and UNDRIP principles into how the system behaves. Testing CEIMIA's roadmap on Indigenous data should push the tool somewhere more inclusive, because community data raises questions government data never does.

It's a small, concrete pilot to help answer a bigger question: can these systems advance Indigenous rights, not just avoid harming them? That's what we're setting out to test. More information on that pilot here.

Want to talk it through? Book an AI conversation and Niiwin demo direct with our CEO Jeff: grab a time.
items.summary: News River Express News River Express is back with three articles worth your time, plus a bit of our own news. We built this summer format so we can spend more time outside with our plants and our people, and still get the best links to your inbox. This week is the topic everyone is talking about:

items.title: [The Verge] Here are the 30,000 songs Sony is suing Udio’s AI music generator over
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Sony Music Entertainment has filed another lawsuit against Udio, accusing the AI music generator of infringing the copyright of more than 30,000 of its songs, ranging from Elvis Presley's Hound Dog to Beyoncé's Say My Name, and Harry Styles' As It Was. The lawsuit, filed in a New York court on Monday, claims that this […]

items.title: [Engadget] Scientists develop handheld device for measuring when your body is burning fat

items.title: [Kottke.org] New orca behavior unlocked. They’ve been known to sink...
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

New orca behavior unlocked. They’ve been known to sink boats and wear salmon for hats; now they’re ramming fish so hard they explode. “Orcas were observed to hold sunfish in their jaws while a second whale smashed into the target at high speed…”

items.title: [The Narwhal] As Canadian politicians promise a carbon emissions fix, the country burns. Here’s what you need to know
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Deadly wildfire seasons — made worse by climate change — are ravaging the country. Politicians from Harper to Carney have for two decades promised carbon capture technology would slow Canada’s contribution to global warming
items.summary: Deadly wildfire seasons — made worse by climate change — are ravaging the country. Politicians from Harper to Carney have for two decades promised carbon capture technology would slow Canada’s contribution to global warming
